---
title: "Projects"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: false
---

```{=html}
<div class="projects-intro">
  <p class="lead">A selection of graduate-level work from my time at the Rollins School of Public Health. Both projects below are part of my Spring 2026 capstone with the Rollins CARE Team, supporting CARE's DREAMS project in southern Malawi.</p>
</div>

<div class="project-card">
  <div class="project-header">
    <h2>DREAMS Malawi: Dietary Diversity Analysis</h2>
    <span class="project-tag">Quantitative Analysis</span>
  </div>

  <p class="project-meta"><i class="fas fa-calendar"></i> January 2026 &ndash; April 2026 &nbsp;|&nbsp; <i class="fas fa-map-marker-alt"></i> Chiradzulu, Phalombe, and Thyolo districts, Malawi</p>

  <p>After an El Ni&ntilde;o-related drought worsened food insecurity in southern Malawi, CARE's DREAMS program combined cash assistance, agricultural support, nutrition services, and protection programming to help vulnerable families. I led the analysis of one core question: did increased access to nutrient-rich foods translate into measurable improvements in what women and young children were actually eating?</p>

  <p>Using DREAMS household survey data from 2024 (baseline) and 2026 (endline) across three drought-affected districts, I ran a repeated cross-sectional analysis with adjusted prevalence ratio models, controlling for district, respondent age, education, marital status, and household size. The two primary outcomes were the Minimum Dietary Diversity for Women (MDD-W) and the Child Minimum Dietary Diversity (MDD) for children 6&ndash;23 months.</p>

  <p><strong>Key finding:</strong> Dietary diversity improved meaningfully across all three districts. Women at endline were roughly twice as likely to meet MDD-W (adjusted PR 2.01, 95% CI 1.67&ndash;2.41), and children were about 3.5 times as likely to meet child MDD (adjusted PR 3.51, 95% CI 2.56&ndash;4.81). Because the study had no control group, results are interpreted as associations rather than causal effects.</p>

  <p><strong>Skills used:</strong> R, prevalence ratio modeling, survey data analysis, repeated cross-sectional design, scientific poster design.</p>

  <div class="project-links">
    <a href="https://github.com/dhk262/pw" class="project-btn"><i class="fab fa-github"></i> Project Repository</a>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="project-card">
  <div class="project-header">
    <h2>Food Access and Nutrition in Eastern Africa: A Literature Review</h2>
    <span class="project-tag">Evidence Synthesis</span>
  </div>

  <p class="project-meta"><i class="fas fa-calendar"></i> Spring 2026 &nbsp;|&nbsp; <i class="fas fa-book"></i> Individual deliverable, Rollins CARE Capstone</p>

  <p>To ground the DREAMS analysis in existing evidence, I conducted an individual literature review on the relationship between access to nutrient-rich foods and nutritional outcomes for women of reproductive age and children under five in Eastern Africa. I designed a PubMed search strategy focused on studies published from 2021 onward, covering dietary diversity, the Minimum Acceptable Diet (MAD), micronutrient intake, anemia, and maternal undernutrition (BMI and MUAC).</p>

  <p>The final review synthesized 10 studies from Ethiopia, Uganda, Tanzania, and East Africa more broadly, including systematic reviews and meta-analyses, a birth cohort, and community-based cross-sectional studies. I organized findings thematically (child nutrition and feeding, maternal diet quality, anemia) and produced a structured evidence summary table noting design, exposure, outcomes, and limitations for each study.</p>

  <p><strong>Key takeaway:</strong> The literature consistently links wider dietary diversity to better nutritional status in both children and women, but most evidence is observational and cross-sectional, and very little is specific to Malawi or to integrated programs like DREAMS. This gap is exactly what the DREAMS analysis was designed to help address.</p>

  <p><strong>Skills used:</strong> Systematic literature search, evidence synthesis, scientific writing, public health research methods.</p>

  <div class="project-links">
    <a href="https://github.com/dhk262/pw" class="project-btn"><i class="fab fa-github"></i> Project Repository</a>
  </div>
</div>
```
